task: I'm embedding Smithers in my own Bun host (not the CLI) and want to capture the live workflow tree, safely serialize arbitrary task props, and compute deltas between two commits. Which npm package exposes the `snapshotSerialize`, `diffSnapshots`, and `applyDelta` helpers, and which two classes does it export to aggregate per-run engine state and per-commit snapshots?

I imported `SmithersDevTools` from the reconciler devtools adapter and constructed it with an `onCommit` handler. Now it needs to actually begin observing React fiber-root commits while my workflow runs, then tear the instrumentation back down on process exit. Which two instance methods begin and end instrumentation?

Given a `SmithersDevToolsCore` instance that has already captured at least one commit (it's been observing a long review workflow), write a small TypeScript snippet that: (1) prints the current Smithers tree to a string for a log line, (2) finds the single node for the task whose nodeId is `review-step`, and (3) lists every task node currently in the tree. Use only the core instance's own methods.
notes: Correct code must use the SmithersDevToolsCore instance methods directly: `core.printTree()` to render the tree to a string, `core.findTask("review-step")` to locate the node by its task nodeId, and `core.listTasks()` to enumerate task nodes (it may also read `core.snapshot` or `core.tree`). It must NOT invent methods like `core.getTree()`, `core.search()`, `core.nodes`, or re-import the free `printTree`/`findNodeById` functions and re-implement what the instance already wraps. Handling the empty case (printTree returns "(no tree captured yet)" when nothing is captured) is a plus, not required.

task: My DevTools snapshots include task props that sometimes contain circular references, very deeply nested objects, more entries than I want to walk, and the odd class instance (a Map, a custom class). I pass an `onWarning` callback to `snapshotSerialize`. What are the four warning `code` values it can report?

`snapshotSerialize` replaced a deeply nested value in my snapshot with the string `[MaxDepth]` even though I never passed a `maxDepth` option. What is the default maximum depth the serializer applies (the `SNAPSHOT_SERIALIZER_DEFAULT_MAX_DEPTH` constant)?

I'm building a live tree viewer that calls `diffSnapshots(prev, next)` between two `DevToolsSnapshotV1` frames and applies the minimal change set. What are the `op` discriminator names that can appear in the returned delta's `ops` array?

A teammate insists `diffSnapshots` emits a `moveNode` op when a task node is reparented or reordered between two frames, and a separate remove+add when the root changes. Is that right? Explain what ops it actually produces for a node that moved, and what single op it uses when the root node's identity or shape changes.
notes: There is NO moveNode op. A node whose parentId or sibling index changed (a move) OR whose shape (type/name/depth) changed (a replace) is encoded as a removeNode of its old id PLUS an addNode at the new parent/index — the diff models move and replace identically as remove+add. When the ROOT's id changed, or the root's shape changed, the diff returns a single `replaceRoot` op (because the root node cannot be removed), not a remove+add pair. A correct answer must reject moveNode, describe remove+add for moved/replaced nodes, and name replaceRoot for the root case.

task: My live viewer applies wire deltas with `applyDelta(snapshot, delta)`, but it throws the moment a frame is skipped or arrives out of order. Which error class does `applyDelta` throw, and which field on the incoming delta must equal the current snapshot's `seq` for the apply to be accepted?

The Gateway DevTools stream sends `DevToolsEvent` frames that are either a full snapshot or a delta. Write a TypeScript function `applyEvent(current: DevToolsSnapshotV1 | null, event: DevToolsEvent): DevToolsSnapshotV1` that updates local state: on a snapshot event adopt it as the new baseline, on a delta event apply it using the devtools helper. Import the helper and types from the real packages.
notes: Must import `applyDelta` from `@smithers-orchestrator/devtools` and the `DevToolsEvent` / snapshot types from `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol` (the protocol devtools module). It must discriminate on `event.kind`: for `"snapshot"` return `event.snapshot` (new baseline); for `"delta"` return `applyDelta(current, event.delta)` while guarding the null / no-baseline case (a delta with no current snapshot can't apply). It must NOT hand-roll tree patching or invent a nonexistent helper like `applyDevToolsEvent`. Recognizing the discriminated union (kind: "snapshot" | "delta") and that applyDelta returns a new snapshot is the core of a correct answer.

task: Every DevTools snapshot, delta, and wire event carries a `version` field so clients can reject incompatible streams. Which constant does `@smithers-orchestrator/protocol` export for this, and what is its current numeric value?

My DevTools gateway client needs an exhaustive switch over `DEVTOOLS_ERROR_CODES` (the versioned error codes the protocol package exports for the devtools snapshot/stream surface). List the seven codes in that array. Note: do not add the generic transport codes the RPC docs page mentions that are not in this constant.
canonicalAnswer: RunNotFound, InvalidRunId, FrameOutOfRange, SeqOutOfRange, BackpressureDisconnect, Unauthorized, InvalidDelta

task: A slow DevTools subscriber keeps its WebSocket open while a hot run produces frames faster than the client drains them. At how many buffered events does `streamDevTools`'s per-subscriber queue give up, and which error code does it raise (and the client receive) when it does?

To bound client drift, the DevTools stream periodically sends a full snapshot instead of another delta (and also snapshots when a delta would be larger than the equivalent snapshot). After how many deltas since the last snapshot does it force a re-baseline snapshot — the `DEVTOOLS_REBASELINE_INTERVAL`?

For `diffSnapshots` to match nodes across frames, each `DevToolsNode.id` must stay stable across React commits for the same fiber (otherwise every node looks removed-and-re-added). Which two bippy helpers does the reconciler devtools adapter use to read an existing per-fiber id and assign one when it's missing?

I want to pretty-print a captured DevTools tree to a string (one indented line per node, each prefixed with a type emoji) so I can dump it into an incident log. Which exported function renders the tree, and which exported icon map does it index by node `type` to pick each line's prefix?

Write a TypeScript snippet that stands up a `DevToolsRunStore`, attaches it to a Smithers `EventBus`-like emitter so it ingests engine events, and then — after some events have flowed — reads the `RunExecutionState` for run `run-42` and the task state for nodeId `build` at iteration `0`. Use the real store API and remember teardown.
notes: Must construct `new DevToolsRunStore()` (options optional), subscribe via `store.attachEventBus(bus)` (which registers a handler on the bus's `event` channel), then read run state with `store.getRun("run-42")` and task state with `store.getTaskState("run-42", "build", 0)` (runId, nodeId, iteration). Feeding events directly via `store.processEngineEvent(event)` is an acceptable alternative to attachEventBus. It should call `store.detachEventBuses()` on teardown. It must NOT invent `store.subscribe()`, `store.runs.find(...)`, or `store.getTask(nodeId)` with the wrong signature. Import `DevToolsRunStore` from `@smithers-orchestrator/devtools`.

task: React DevTools has to hook in before any React/reconciler module is imported, or it misses the renderer registration. The devtools package ships a preload script that calls `installRDTHook()`. Which Bun CLI flag runs that script first, and which bunfig.toml key configures the same thing for every run?

The `SmithersDevTools` adapter taps React commits without React DOM. When it instruments, which two bippy functions does it use to wrap and securely register its commit callbacks, and which global-hook handler does it override to capture each fiber-root commit?

In the DevTools tree a `<Loop>` component shows up as a node whose `type` is `"loop"`. The classifier only matches host fibers via `HOST_TAG_MAP`. What is the underlying React host-tag string the reconciler emits for that component that the map keys to `"loop"`?

Author a Smithers workflow whose DevTools tree (the one printTree would render) contains, besides the `workflow` root, these distinct node types: a worktree, a parallel block, a loop, and an approval gate — with at least one agent `<Task>` somewhere inside. So the classifier reports worktree, parallel, loop, and approval node types for your tree. Write the workflow component.

I'm reading `TaskExecutionState.status` from the DevTools run store to draw per-task badges. Besides the terminal values (`finished`, `failed`, `cancelled`, `skipped`) and the active ones (`pending`, `started`), what are the three `waiting-*` statuses a task can hold, and which status marks an in-progress retry?

To make a `DevToolsRunStore` (or the `SmithersDevToolsCore` wrapping it) ingest engine events from a Smithers `EventBus`, which method do I call to subscribe, and which event-channel name string does it pass to `bus.on(...)` under the hood?

In the DevTools run store, a run flipped to `waiting-approval` when one task raised NodeWaitingApproval; a second task is parked on `waiting-timer`. The timer task then resumes (a NodeStarted event arrives for it). What does the store set the RUN-level status to, and what rule decides which waiting status the run shows while multiple tasks are blocked? Also: can this logic ever overwrite a `finished` run?
notes: When a task leaves a waiting state, the store recomputes the run's waiting status (refreshRunWaitingStatus), but ONLY when the run is currently parked in a run-level waiting status (waiting-approval / waiting-timer) and is NOT terminal. It scans all tasks: if any task is still `waiting-approval`, the run stays `waiting-approval` (approval is the strongest block and overrides timer); otherwise if any task is `waiting-timer`, the run is `waiting-timer`; if none are blocking, the run resumes to `running`. So here the run STAYS `waiting-approval` (the approval task is still waiting) — it does not flip to running just because the timer task resumed. Terminal statuses (finished/failed/cancelled) are never overwritten by this logic.

task: The DevTools run store records each agent tool call on `TaskExecutionState.toolCalls`. Which two engine event types push a new entry and then resolve its outcome, matched by `toolName` + `seq`, and which two `status` values can a finished tool call carry?
